The man understood from his saying "These are in Paradise and I do not mind, and these are in the Fire and I do not mind" the negation of choice (الاختيار). So he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) answered him that this is a decree (قدر) from Him, the Exalted, and that our deeds—while we are the ones doing them and they are attributed to us—fall upon what the decree falls upon. Thus, they correspond to the decree and the decree corresponds to them. This is because Allah decreed what He decreed through the path of our choice; so we act by our choice, yet what Allah, the Glorified, decreed occurs. It is not that He, the Exalted, nullified our choice through destiny or negated the effect of our will. The narrations with this meaning are numerous.

In *al-Kafi*, from Ali ibn Ibrahim, from his father, from Ibn Abi Umayr, from Ibn Udhaynah, from Zurarah, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "I asked him about the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: **حُنَفاءَ لِلَّهِ غَيْرَ مُشْرِكِينَ** 'upright (حنفاء) — *those who incline toward the true religion and turn away from all falsehood* — before Allah, not ascribing partners to Him' (22:31)." He said: "The uprightness (الحنفية) is from the primordial nature (الفطرة) according to which He originated mankind—there is no change in Allah's creation. He said: 'He originated them upon the knowledge (المعرفة) of Him.'"

Zurarah said: "And I asked him about the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: **وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَأَشْهَدَهُمْ عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ قالُوا بَلى** the verse. He said: 'He extracted from the back of Adam his progeny until the Day of Resurrection. They emerged like small ants (الذر), so He made Himself known to them and showed them Himself. Were it not for that, no one would recognize his Lord.'"

And he said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Every newborn is born upon the primordial nature (الفطرة)'—meaning upon the knowledge (المعرفة) that Allah, Mighty and Majestic, is his Creator. Likewise is His saying: **وَلَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ اللهُ** 'If you ask them, 'Who created the heavens and the earth?' they will surely say, 'Allah'' (31:25)."

**I say:** Al-Ayyashi narrated the middle of the hadith in his *Tafsir* from Zurarah with the exact same wording. In it is a testimony to what was previously established regarding the meaning of the witnessing (الإشهاد) and the address in the verse, contrary to what was mentioned by those who deny it—that what is meant by it is the knowledge through the signs indicating His Lordship, the Exalted, over all His creation.

The hadith was narrated in *al-Ma'ani* with the same chain from Zurarah from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), except that it said: "He made them recognize and showed them His handiwork (صنعه)" instead of his saying: "He made Himself known to them and showed them Himself." Perhaps this is a deliberate change of wording for the purpose of transmission by meaning (النقل بالمعنى), under the assumption that the apparent wording suggests anthropomorphism (التجسم). In this is the corruption of both the wording and the meaning. You have seen that the narration is recorded in *al-Kafi* and *Tafsir al-Ayyashi* with the wording: "He showed them Himself."

It was previously mentioned in the hadith of Ibn Muskan from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) his saying: "I said: 'Was this a direct vision (معاينة)?' He said: 'Yes.'" It has already been stated that the speech has no connection to the issue of anthropomorphism.

In *al-Mahasin*, from al-Hasan ibn Ali ibn Faddal, from Ibn Bukayr, from Zurarah, who said: "I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah: **وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ** the verse. He said: 'The knowledge (المعرفة) was established in their hearts, but they forgot the occasion (الموقف), and they shall remember it one day. Were it not for that, no one would know who his Creator and Sustainer is.'"

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him) saw no harm in coitus interruptus (العزل). He would read this verse: **وَإِذْ أَخَذَ رَبُّكَ مِنْ بَنِي آدَمَ مِنْ ظُهُورِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَأَشْهَدَهُمْ عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ قالُوا بَلى**. So everything from which Allah has taken the covenant (الميثاق) is bound to emerge, even if it were upon a solid rock."

**I say:** It was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Abi Shaybah and Ibn Jarir from him (peace be upon him). This meaning was also narrated from Sa'id ibn Mansur and Ibn Mardawayh from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله).

Know that the narrations regarding the small ants (الذر) are very numerous. We have omitted most of them because what we have cited sufficiently conveys their meaning. There are other narrations regarding the taking of the covenant from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the rest of the prophets (peace be upon them), which we shall cite in their proper place, if Allah, the Exalted, wills.
